description: Drop-in CLI binary (codemux) + companion Zed extension that opens Zed terminals inside tmux/zellij â€” port of vscode-mux to Zed. Mirrors vscode-mux session-naming and multi-window indexing exactly so the same workspace opened in VS Code and Zed lands in the same multiplexer session.

userStories.title: Scaffold the Rust binary crate
userStories.description: As a developer, I need a working Rust crate so subsequent stories have a place to add code.
acceptanceCriteria: Create Cargo.toml at repo root with package name 'codemux', edition 2021, [[bin]] target named 'codemux'
Add baseline dependencies in Cargo.toml: clap (with derive feature), which, serde + serde_derive, toml, anyhow
Create src/main.rs with a minimal entry point that prints 'codemux' and exits 0
Add .gitignore covering /target, Cargo.lock for binaries decision, .DS_Store
Add LICENSE file (MIT) at repo root with year 2026 and author 'jellydn'
`cargo build` succeeds
`cargo run -- --version` does not panic (a placeholder is fine for now)
Typecheck passes (cargo check passes)

userStories.title: Implement session-name sanitization with vscode-mux fixture tests
userStories.description: As the codemux binary, I need to convert any workspace name into a tmux/zellij-safe session name using the exact vscode-mux algorithm so cross-editor session sharing works.
acceptanceCriteria: Add src/sanitize.rs (or module) with `pub fn sanitize_session_name(name: &str) -> String`
Algorithm exactly matches vscode-mux: replace any char not in [a-zA-Z0-9-] with '-', collapse consecutive '-' into one, strip leading/trailing '-', return literal 'session' if result is empty
Use the regex crate (add to Cargo.toml) or hand-rolled char iteration

Unit tests cover the vscode-mux fixture table: 'My Workspace' â†’ 'My-Workspace', 'my.project' â†’ 'my-project', 'my_project' â†’ 'my-project', 'my...project' â†’ 'my-project', '-myproject-' â†’ 'myproject', '...' â†’ 'session', '' â†’ 'session'
All unit tests pass (`cargo test sanitize`)
Typecheck passes (cargo check passes)
Tests pass

userStories.title: Implement POSIX shell-escape helper with tests
userStories.description: As the codemux binary, I need a shell-escape function (matching vscode-mux exactly) so session names and cwd values are safely interpolated when building shell commands.
acceptanceCriteria: Add src/shell_escape.rs with `pub fn shell_escape(value: &str) -> String`
If input is empty, return literal `''`
Otherwise wrap in single quotes and replace internal `'` with `'"'"'` (POSIX single-quote escaping)
Unit tests: '' â†’ "''", 'foo' â†’ "'foo'", "it's" â†’ "'it'\"'\"'s'", '/path with spaces' â†’ "'/path with spaces'"
All unit tests pass (`cargo test shell_escape`)
Typecheck passes
Tests pass

userStories.title: Implement TOML config-file loader
userStories.description: As the codemux binary, I need to read ~/.config/codemux/config.toml so users can persist multiplexer choice and auto_attach.
acceptanceCriteria: Add src/config.rs with a `Config` struct: `multiplexer: Option<String>`, `auto_attach: Option<bool>`
Function `pub fn load_config() -> Config` reads `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/codemux/config.toml` or `~/.config/codemux/config.toml` (Windows: `%APPDATA%/codemux/config.toml`)
Returns default `Config { multiplexer: None, auto_attach: None }` if file is missing or unreadable (no panic)
Use `dirs` crate for cross-platform config dir resolution (add to Cargo.toml)
Unit tests: parse a valid TOML string, parse an empty string (returns defaults), parse invalid TOML (returns defaults, does not panic)
Tests use a `parse_config_str(&str)` helper so they don't touch the filesystem
Typecheck passes
Tests pass

userStories.title: Implement multiplexer detection (env â†’ config â†’ PATH)
userStories.description: As the codemux binary, I need to decide which multiplexer to use, in the priority order from the PRD.
acceptanceCriteria: Add src/detect.rs with `pub enum Multiplexer { Tmux, Zellij }` and `pub fn detect_multiplexer(config: &Config) -> Option<Multiplexer>`
Resolution order: 1) env var `CODEMUX_MULTIPLEXER` ('tmux' | 'zellij'), 2) config.multiplexer field, 3) PATH probe via `which` crate â€” prefer 'tmux', then 'zellij'
Returns `None` only when none of the above resolves (PATH probe found nothing AND no override)
Unit tests inject env vars and config to verify resolution priority; a test for the PATH-probe path can be `#[ignore]` if it depends on host state
Typecheck passes
Tests pass

userStories.title: Implement multiplexer launcher trait + tmux session listing
userStories.description: As the codemux binary, I need an abstraction for multiplexer operations and a tmux implementation that can list live sessions.
acceptanceCriteria: Add src/launcher.rs with `pub trait MuxLauncher { fn list_sessions(&self) -> anyhow::Result<Vec<String>>; fn build_command(&self, name: &str, cwd: &str, auto_attach: bool) -> String; }`
Add src/tmux.rs implementing `MuxLauncher` for `TmuxLauncher`
`list_sessions` runs `tmux list-sessions -F '#{session_name}'`, captures stdout, splits by newline, trims, filters empty lines
If tmux exits with non-zero status because no server is running, treat as empty session list (do not error)
`build_command` matches vscode-mux: auto-attach mode â†’ `tmux new-session -A -s <escaped-name> -c <escaped-cwd>`; otherwise â†’ `tmux new-session -s <escaped-name> -c <escaped-cwd>`
Unit test for `build_command` covers both auto-attach values; uses fixed name + cwd
Typecheck passes
Tests pass

userStories.title: Implement zellij launcher (list + build_command)
userStories.description: As the codemux binary, I need a zellij implementation of MuxLauncher matching vscode-mux's exact commands.
acceptanceCriteria: Add src/zellij.rs implementing `MuxLauncher` for `ZellijLauncher`
`list_sessions` runs `zellij list-sessions -n`, captures stdout, splits/trims, filters empty
If zellij exits non-zero because no sessions exist, return empty list (do not error)
`build_command`: auto-attach â†’ `zellij attach <escaped-name> -c`; otherwise â†’ `zellij -s <escaped-name>` (NO -c <cwd> in either form â€” matches vscode-mux behavior)
Unit tests for `build_command` cover both auto-attach values
Typecheck passes
Tests pass

userStories.title: Implement getUniqueSessionName with gap-filling
userStories.description: As the codemux binary, I need to compute a unique suffixed session name using the exact vscode-mux algorithm so multi-window scenarios behave identically across editors.
acceptanceCriteria: Add `pub fn get_unique_session_name(base: &str, sessions: &[String]) -> String` (pure function â€” takes the session list as input so it is trivially testable)
If `base` is not in `sessions`, return `base` unchanged
Otherwise start `suffix = 2`; while `format!("{base}-{suffix}")` is in `sessions`, `suffix += 1`; return `format!("{base}-{suffix}")`
Unit tests cover the PRD table: [] â†’ 'myapp', ['myapp'] â†’ 'myapp-2', ['myapp','myapp-2'] â†’ 'myapp-3', ['myapp','myapp-2','myapp-5'] â†’ 'myapp-3' (gap-fill)
Typecheck passes
Tests pass

userStories.title: Wire main entry point: detect, name, dispatch
userStories.description: As a Zed user, I want `codemux` to detect the multiplexer, compute a session name from CWD, and exec into the multiplexer command.
acceptanceCriteria: src/main.rs reads CWD via `std::env::current_dir()`, computes basename â†’ `sanitize_session_name`
Loads config (US-004), detects multiplexer (US-005)
Resolves auto_attach: env `CODEMUX_AUTO_ATTACH` ('true'|'false') overrides config.auto_attach overrides default `true`
If auto_attach is true and base name already exists in `list_sessions`, use base name; otherwise call `get_unique_session_name` to pick a new name
Builds command string via launcher's `build_command`, then `exec`s it via `$SHELL -l -c <command>` on Unix (using `std::os::unix::process::CommandExt::exec`), or spawn+wait on Windows
On Windows, use `cmd.exe /C <command>` (or PowerShell if `$SHELL` is set) and propagate the child exit code
Manual smoke test: running `codemux` in a directory called `test-proj` while tmux is installed lands in a tmux session named `test-proj` (document this in NOTES file or PR description)
Typecheck passes
Tests pass
userStories.notes: Main entry point fully implemented with auto_attach resolution, session name selection, command building, and cross-platform exec/spawn

userStories.title: Graceful fallback when no multiplexer is installed
userStories.description: As a user without tmux/zellij, I want codemux to still give me a usable terminal with one helpful warning.
acceptanceCriteria: When `detect_multiplexer` returns `None`, print a single-line warning to stderr: `codemux: tmux/zellij not found on PATH â€” falling back to $SHELL. Install tmux or zellij to enable multiplexer mode.`
Exec the user's default shell: Unix â†’ `$SHELL` env var, fallback `/bin/sh`; Windows â†’ `%COMSPEC%`, fallback `cmd.exe`
Process is replaced via `exec` on Unix; on Windows spawn+wait and propagate exit code
Unit test (or integration test) verifies the warning string and the chosen shell path given a mocked env (use a small `decide_fallback_shell()` helper that takes env as input)
Typecheck passes
Tests pass
userStories.notes: Fallback shell implementation complete with warning message, decide_fallback_shell helper, and unit tests

userStories.title: Add CLI flags: --version, --help, CODEMUX_DEBUG logging
userStories.description: As a user, I want standard CLI ergonomics and an opt-in debug mode for troubleshooting.
acceptanceCriteria: Use `clap` derive API to define a `Cli` struct with `--version` and `--help` (auto-generated)
All other args are forwarded as-is to the spawned shell command (do not error on unknown args)
When env `CODEMUX_DEBUG=1` is set, log the resolved multiplexer, base name, sanitized name, final session name, and full command to stderr â€” prefix each line with `[codemux] `
When `CODEMUX_DEBUG` is unset or anything other than `1`, no debug output
Unit test for the debug-decision helper (`fn debug_enabled(env: &HashMap<String,String>) -> bool`)
Typecheck passes
Tests pass
userStories.notes: CLI struct with clap derive, --version and --help working, CODEMUX_DEBUG logging implemented with 5 unit tests

userStories.title: Add GitHub Actions CI workflow
userStories.description: As a maintainer, I want CI to verify build, lint, format, and tests on macOS, Linux, and Windows.
acceptanceCriteria: Create `.github/workflows/ci.yml` with a job matrix: `os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest]`
Job runs: checkout, setup-rust (stable + clippy + rustfmt), `cargo build --release`, `cargo test`, `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`, `cargo fmt --check`
Cache cargo registry + target dir across runs (use `Swatinem/rust-cache` action)
Workflow runs on push to main and pull_request
Verify locally: `cargo build --release && cargo test && cargo clippy -- -D warnings && cargo fmt --check` all succeed
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: CI workflow created with matrix for ubuntu-latest, macos-latest, windows-latest. Uses actions/checkout@v4, dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable, and Swatinem/rust-cache@v2. All quality checks pass locally.

userStories.title: Add release workflow producing cross-platform binaries
userStories.description: As a user, I want prebuilt binaries downloadable from GitHub Releases so I don't have to install Rust.
acceptanceCriteria: Create `.github/workflows/release.yml` triggered on tag push matching `v*`
Build matrix produces: macos-x64, macos-arm64, linux-x64, linux-arm64, windows-x64
Each artifact is a tar.gz (or .zip on Windows) named `codemux-<version>-<target>.{tar.gz,zip}` containing the `codemux` binary
Workflow creates a GitHub Release with `softprops/action-gh-release` and attaches all artifacts
Workflow lints OK with `actionlint` (or at minimum `yamllint` passes)
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Release workflow created with 5-target build matrix. Uses actions/checkout@v4, dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable, Swatinem/rust-cache@v2, actions/upload-artifact@v4, softprops/action-gh-release@v2. Artifacts: codemux-{version}-macos-x64.tar.gz, codemux-{version}-macos-arm64.tar.gz, codemux-{version}-linux-x64.tar.gz, codemux-{version}-linux-arm64.tar.gz, codemux-{version}-windows-x64.zip

userStories.title: Add Zed companion extension manifest + license
userStories.description: As a Zed user, I want CodeMux to be installable from Zed's extension registry so I can discover it the standard way.
acceptanceCriteria: Create `extension.toml` at repo root with: id='codemux', name='CodeMux', version='0.1.0', schema_version=1, authors=['jellydn'], description='Open Zed terminals inside tmux or zellij â€” port of vscode-mux to Zed.', repository='https://github.com/jellydn/zed-codemux'
License file at repo root is named `LICENSE` and contains an accepted Zed license (MIT) â€” already present from US-001 but verify
Do NOT bundle the `codemux` binary in the extension package (per Zed policy)
README has an explicit section on installing the binary alongside the extension
Add `tasks/zed-extension-submission.md` with a step-by-step description of how to fork zed-industries/extensions, add the new entry, and open the PR (this story does not actually open the PR)
Typecheck passes (cargo check still succeeds)
userStories.notes: extension.toml created with correct metadata, LICENSE verified (MIT), README has binary install section, zed-extension-submission.md created with step-by-step PR instructions

userStories.title: Polish README and add usage GIF placeholder
userStories.description: As a new user, I want a README that walks me through install â†’ configure â†’ first terminal.
acceptanceCriteria: Verify README.md at repo root covers: Why â†’ Features â†’ Install (binary + extension) â†’ Configure (settings.json snippet) â†’ Configuration (env + config.toml) â†’ Session naming table â†’ Multiplexer commands table â†’ Requirements â†’ Roadmap â†’ Credits â†’ License
Add an `assets/` folder containing a placeholder `demo.gif` (1Ă—1 transparent PNG renamed is fine if no real GIF yet) and reference it from the README's Why section
Add a 'Differences from vscode-mux' subsection enumerating: no Zed extension API for terminal profiles â†’ CLI binary approach; no `kill` subcommand in v1; no per-workspace config in v1
Add a 'Compared to vscode-mux' link block at the top of the README
Markdown lints OK (`markdownlint-cli` or eyeball check â€” no broken links)
